pages.text: thank you very much everyone um welcome to our second uh town council meeting of the year uh madam clerk this is to inform oh okay this is to inform the general public that this meeting is being held in compliance with section 5 of the open public meetings act chapter 231 public law 1975. the annual notice was emailed to the star ledger and filed in the township clerk's office on november 16 2020 and published in the west orange chronicle on november 25th 2020 councilwoman casolino present councilwoman mccartney presents councilman rutherford rosie councilwoman williams president council president matthew brown president thank you very much madam clerk and we are going to start our conference agenda today with a presentation uh from our library board president as well as dave qb who is our library director and we're also joined by anthony iovino who is the architect who's worked on the project for the library um and i'm not sure who's starting marge 32 00:01:22,24

are you starting excuse me yes i am going to start thank you so um thank you good evening um madam president hate brown and good evening to all our council members so thank you for giving us this time to present plans for a new library and the many opportunities for new services it will afford the residents of west orange um as uh council president um just mentioned i will be joined by our director dave cooby and our architect anthony iovino so the first slide is what are the advantages and opportunities that afford our residents one of the things if you're if you're familiar with real estate you know that location location location is so important and the library took this into consideration we are geographically centered in the township which is convenient for all of our residents for those who are in the outliers there is a jitney service the new location is close to shoprite

and that's another advantage this is a green project a green environment project we have ample parking for over 300 cars which is a huge difference we only have 32 spaces presently and the space allows for reclamation of trees and gardens which will enhance it quite a bit and we have outdoor space for we call a black box theater which is a presentation of plays and programs that can be done outdoors and we're utilizing an existing building which is really green we're not starting from scratch it's a bigger better healthier space environment with all new hvac and ductwork system with quality air care we have our main programming space which doubles to 150 seats and there is a separate entrance from the outside which is a huge uh difference from what we have now um there's 28 percent increase in usable flexible space which is something very um important because we change whatever programming that we're doing

3:48,959 the children's area is two and a half times larger with a dedicated programming room for parents who bring their young children to the children's room you know that's a big advantage where you can actually stay someone in the background and watch what's going on we have teen space which is four times larger we have quiet room space uh for conferencing and we have instructional space so um that's very important too one thing we don't have now is a separate room for a server and it services which is really required so it's temperature controlled and we will have a separate room for our west orange historical archives maybe the most important thing too besides parking i view as additional technology which we don't have interactive presentations which we will be able to do laptops for checkouts online instructions from universities virtual classes which will enhance it and our financial benefits

um it's only 203 dollars per square footage and our library construction act bond which was approved in november provides almost 3 million 100 000 uh by our township's partnership with private developers for 2.5 million and an additional 598 thousand dollars funds that were already approved by the township for improvements in 46 mount pleasant so keep in mind that these funds cannot be used for any other facility including our present library and um we will talk about the other advantages for the location so lauren the next slide and dave dave you're actually muted right now one of the concerns that we had when we were investigating when the proposition was put before us was what would be the advantages to the community one of my experiences as a library director is that the more convenient the library location is is the greater the community's engagement will be with it i've created two green circles on this which are 162 00:06:27

uh encircle uh both the 46 mount pleasant site which is you know our current location as well as the proposed site of 10 rooney circle what the circles indicate is uh is those circles are one mile across um and the reason i did that is there is a study that was done by the national institute of health which we have if you go to our website www.wopl.org we have all of these documents on the page so you can explore further and of course you can always reach us and ask more questions um is that what this study indicated is that people don't walk more than 0.5 to 0.7 miles on average to go anywhere and so and the 0.7 represents recreational walking such as with your dog so i wanted to point that out for the size of west orange if you could back out just a little bit for the size of west orange um you'll see that there is no single location that could make that is walkable or easily accessible by anything other than

re's um always the need to look for revenue sources especially in new jersey the way government is structured in new jersey other than property taxes we don't really get off opportunities to raise money um so we're always looking for ways to do that and we've been working with pearl media over the last couple of months and what we think is an innovative way to create another revenue source by tying in our jitneys and the bus shelters that are around town as well as a few other components we think it's a very interesting approach and uh bring some benefits to the community and we've um we think we put a pretty good proposal together so i'm grateful for the opportunity to present to the council tonight i do want to introduce pearl media josh and jay are with us tonight and i think ryan shipman is also with us they've worked hard to come up with this presentation i think it's um innovative speaks to their expertise and their success of doing this

that could come in the form of billboards that can come in the form as you see here in south orange on the train station behind the sopak the sopak movie theater and what we do is we come up with a very unique plan working with the city along with your rules and regulations to figure out ways that we can monetize your assets by creating really clever distinctive media assets on city owned or private citizen-owned properties to create communicated messaging but at the same time an opportunity to drive revenue combined at pro media um you know i started this business 15 years ago just a little quick antidote i grew up in west orange i went to pleasantdale elementary school so i'm familiar with the town and this is something that uh i hold a little bit dear to my heart so i'm excited to be here today and i'm hopeful that we can figure out a way to make something like this work what we do um this is a zero cost

oice for your local west orange businesses um is a significant opportunity for the township of west orange so what we are proposing is an all-encompassing media platform and it starts with creating and developing unique digital signs throughout the town we have an opportunity to work on your jitney buses to create to not only add what we think is take something which is a little bit uh old and somewhat of an eyesore at the moment and be a little bit creative with it and allow a brand to sponsor it to create uh drive additional money to that service and then the bus shelter program which we'll get into a little bit later but the best shelter program can definitely use some refreshments and an opportunity to allow brands to really engage with the consumers on the street in the town of west orange so our approach is a little bit different than some other folks when we look at developing signage networks inside inside of towns or in cities we're not

meetings.id: 2026-07-17-Township Council Workshop  Subdivision of Rock Spring Golf Club April 5, 2021.en

pages.text: this is to inform the general public that this meeting is being held in compliance with section 5 of the open public meetings act chapter 231 public law 1975. the notice for this meeting was emailed to the star ledger and the west orange chronicle and filed in the township clerk's office on march 18 2021 councilwoman casolino present councilwoman mccartney as in councilman rutherford councilwoman williams council president matthew brown president mayor parisi president thank you madam clerk and thank you to everyone who is here and joining us for the mayor's presentation on uh rock spring and without any further ado mr mayor thank you council president thank you for um for giving us the time and i know it's been a long time coming but there's a lot of pieces to this that we're going to talk a little bit about long presentation so i wish that uh you know hope everyone can bear with us and we'll obviously spend as much time as the council wo

uld like us to to answer questions and discuss afterwards so i am going to share and obviously confirm that uh everyone can you can see this okay can everyone see that yes we're good we're good wonderful hopefully the public can as well so again thank you council president for this opportunity to talk about rock spring this is a been a long time coming and there's there's a few reasons for that we're going to take you through the process kind of take you through what we're thinking and where we think we go from here and and hopefully the council uh you know we could address their questions and hopefully the council we can all come to some agreement on where we go so i do want to just take one little side step here this was a given to me by david a pretty neat little thing um this is the original architectural rendering of the course it was uh of all places found in the maintenance shed at rock spring this document we're looking at as you can see it's pretty uh pr

etty badly torn and worn but it's uh it's date back 100 years to the club's original opening um so we didn't scrutinize the course layout but this is uh this document uh speaks to the history of the course again opening originally in 1925 uh just a little neat artifact that i thought we'd kind of show uh the public unfortunately it's not in good enough shape to display somewhere but still pretty cool so just as a summary the um some of these things so the course was originally purchased again after lengthy conversation we closed on april 18th of 2019 the original purchase price was a little over 11 million dollars the property generated at the time property tax is just under 600 000 part of the discussion and the mathematics that went into the decision was that we were going to forego those taxes we knew that but that is something that needs to be addressed overall beginning in 2022 we are going to have principal and interest payments that the township 95 00:03:1

council will have to deal with in their budget process of roughly 550 to 850 thousand dollars i know it's a long vig and john gross can talk about that a little bit later on that has to do with whether or not there's gonna be short-term notes or long-term notes the council can discuss that probably at a later date maybe the budget period we can talk about that but we are going to have principal and interest payments we have to we have to address i do want to acknowledge david cronheim who was part of the discussions with the township when we first purchased the course when this conversation started a couple years ago he's been a wonderful asset to the community and obviously with the council support we connected with kyle our golf architect who's been again a wonderful resource not only has he had some experience with rockspring specifically but experience with golf courses all over the country and their expertise and the supporting staff from the

two of them has obviously been important to us in getting to this point tonight and obviously the township professional staff as well uh all the same people that the council deals with on an ongoing basis lenny and jack and john gross and the chief and nick who also besides being oem and dealing with the pandemic this past year has been very helpful with the rock spring as well and of course kemper sports we'll talk about them in a little bit several members from kemper are here tonight they've been a a great partner with the community really really pleased with what they do and they've obviously been very important to the process that we're talking about tonight and obviously you know we're here to talk about what we do with the property long term and and clearly that has a lot to do with development and i can tell you we've heard and discussed this property a lot with um with the development community both solicited and unsolicited once this kind of hit the news uh in 2019 you know ever since then people have been reaching out wi

th their thoughts and their interests so we have not had any shortage of feedback about the property and a lot of that helped us figure out what uh what it is we're going to talk about tonight obviously again i talked about kemper just really wonderful at what they do professional been a pleasure uh to work with and uh the course in the first two seasons have been has been a huge success despite some challenges and one of those challenges well obviously in 2019 we didn't close until i i mentioned earlier april so we didn't have the first rounds until may 10th and yet in 2019 they still did over 22 000 rounds of golf in that first season that the township owned the course and 2020 again challenges mainly because of covid but yet they still did over 35 almost 36 000 rounds of golf way more than a private club had done and obviously we knew that a public course uh would would do more than a private clu

b did but but way more successful than i think though we had been hopeful that the public would embrace the course they clearly have and uh the first two years have have really shown the public's interest in the course right now there's just under 14 000 golf customers in the database and what's interesting about that is a little over twelve thousand a huge majority of that number are actually repeat golf customers meaning that people come they like it and they're coming back and coming back and coming back uh if you look at the top five zip codes no big surprise that west orange would be the number one zip code in terms of 20 of the users are from west orange that's really not a surprise for any public course and livingston nearby and mafia fairfield area close behind and and hoboken jersey city i think that's important to mention um as we've heard from kemper over and over from the beginning that this has been really embraced from

that area of the state um which has a lot of people who are working in new york and have been finding opportunities to play in that area or even in new york and they have uh really embraced rock spring and are coming back frequently to the point where that zip code is the fourth highest among our our regular users and then again another essex county area hanover caldwell you know all within close proximity to each other they're both in it at 6.8 so the course has been well received by the public and that was again though we were hopeful it was a big question we had early on as to whether or not the public would embrace this as a public course and clearly in these first two years uh they have so if you look at the revenues and remember the arrangement we made with kemper the township was committed that there would not be any downside we did not want to put the township at risk it was enough of a challenge

50,160 and enough of kind of a unique situation that we were to buy the course we didn't want to be on the hook uh for the operation so the arrangement we made with kemper was township gets five percent of the gross so we didn't want to hear if they had a bad year or if things weren't going well we get five percent of the growth so there was no downside in the first year five percent paid just a little over 85 000 then the expenses come out and then kemper depending on expenses get there five percent and and then we would split anything in excess of that and obviously in that first year probably because we started so late if you remember also uh in 2019 not only did we start late but if you recall that may and june was really wet so they lost i can't tell you how many days of available golf simply because of tremendous rain we had that year so in the end at the end of 2019 there was no there was no profits quote unquote to split but the town did get its its five percent fee of a little over 85 278 0

000 and then in 2020 again the rounds went way up that meant the gross revenue went way up so the five percent of that was a much bigger number to the township and then if you look at we'll talk about this in a second but the expenses were way down so five percent to kemper was much higher and then the township's 50 split became a huge number so in 2020 we generated 540 000 uh in revenue from this course but uh it's just important to kind of pause for a second and not get excited that we're gonna be making six seven hundred thousand dollars every year on this that is largely in part if you look at the expenses the expenses went down even though the gross revenues went up and and that's clearly because of coving because of covid they hired a lot less staff there was not dining staff so there was no food and beverage element uh so the expenses were way down simply because they were running at um you know contactless golf 309 00:09:34,640 -

-> 00:09:37,519 as much as possible so there was much more to split i don't think if 2020 produces the same gross revenue it's unfortunate just not realistic to think that the expenses are going to continue to go down and there's going to be a lot less a lot more for us to split at the end of the year so 2020 great year townships take from rock spring was 542 thousand dollars that's great i just don't want people to think that that's going to be the normal take in the routine take into the future a couple important improvements that needed uh for the course certainly to preserve the course long term and to guarantee long-term play as you can appreciate when you're doing this kind of numbers of rounds it can beat up to beat up the course so it's going to require some improvements and a couple immediately that we have to talk about are the card paths and for those who might not play golf or might not be familiar with it it's for a public course it's important that car

t paths are in good shape and connect from hole to hold to hold the hole so on wet days the cart path can still take someone around all 18 holes but never drive on the grass and the reason that that's important is because a lot of people who enjoy playing golf don't like to walk so kemper has explained from the beginning that because the car pants aren't in good shape when we have rainy days or even days after rainy days and the course is set up where cart paths where the carts can't go on the course and it's walking only a lot of people come and leave so with completing the cart paths and having all 18 holes connected we anticipate with with david and kyle's help as well as kemper's input that the course could probably do an extra three to three point five thousand rounds a year that's significant revenue that we're leaving on the table because when people get there if the course is wet they simply don't want to walk now that's a b

ig investment we anticipate roughly 770 thousand dollars but uh car pass don't uh erode as quickly as roads do so that that is an investment that would last many many years and be in good shape but that is something that's important to consider and and the bunkers we knew when we bought the course that the bunkers were in bad shape rock spring had done some bunker repair over the last 15 years or so some of it was done well some of it wasn't some of it even that was done well had eroded and is in really bad shape and again for golfers that's an important element of the game and we believe the bunker repairs that are needed uh total roughly 750 000 so we know those are big numbers but they are important to the to the play and to the long-term success of the course and we do believe that uh with a long-term operators agreement you know up to now we've only done short-term deals with kemper we believe that with a long-term deal either

meetings.id: 2026-07-17-Town Council Meeting April 20, 2021 West Orange, NJ.en

pages.text: this is to inform the general public that this meeting is being held in compliance with section 5 of the open public's meetings act chapter 231 public law 1975. the annual notice was emailed to the star ledger and filed in the township clerk's office on november 30th 2020 and published in the west orange chronicle on december 10 2020. councilwoman casolino president councilwoman mccartney prison councilman rutherford president councilwoman williams president council president matthew brown president mayor parisi thank you madam clerk and welcome west orange to our tuesday april 20th um meeting of the town council we are going to start with a presentation by our mayor discussing the era in need of rehabilitation i'm saying it that way so that we're not confused and i'm not confused it's an area in need of rehabilitation and i will give the floor to the mayor for him to um discuss this with us mr mayor cav's president thank you 33 00

for giving us the opportunity to speak a little bit about this tonight i apologize about some of the technical issues uh that delayed the start of the meeting that's the administration's fault for not getting that straightened out ahead of time so we appreciate your patience and obviously those that are with us tonight their patients as well so one of the things that came out of the month and months of investigation the administration did with looking at options as we'll continue to look at options for rock spring is we learned a little bit more about the idea of an area in need of rehabilitation and discovered that number of towns around the state have actually implemented a town-wide area in need of rehabilitation we believe it's something that we would like the council to consider and i'm grateful that our planner paul greigel who provided us a lot of information about that during that process is with us tonight to to present to the

council to talk a little bit about what it means and what some of the advantages uh would be to our residents and as always council president we appreciate uh the time on the agenda and the council's consideration so uh i introduce paul to talk uh to talk about this and um and obviously we're here to answer any questions uh the council might have in in deciding if we want to pursue this so paul thank you for being with us tonight and uh i turn it over to you you're welcome thank you mayor it's a pleasure to be here uh council president council members and everyone else in attendance so good evening as the mayor previously mentioned paul greigel been the township's consulting planning director uh since 2012 and i assist the planning board zoning board of adjustment and the council periodically with planning matters but i also just briefly in terms of my practice go work for other municipalities throughout the state as well as property owners and developers and i'

ve assisted with a number of redevelopment and rehabilitation studies as well as redevelopment plans so i'm just going to share briefly some information about this as the mayor mentioned for your consideration the new jersey local redevelopment and housing law is the enabling legislation for redevelopment as well as rehabilitation it provides a number of powers to municipalities in terms of these this topic uh west orange has already taken advantage of some of these notably area needed rehabilitation excuse me redevelopment uh studies for various areas as well as redevelopment plans from multiple locations throughout the township including downtown and more recently some other sites and areas outside of the downtown area as was mentioned other municipalities have utilized the area in need of rehabilitation but west orange has not yet uh this essentially what it is is a designation that can range from a single property to multiple properties

,200 to as was mentioned the entire municipality in fact the local redevelopment housing law was amended in 2014 to change the language to make it's clear that you can designate the entirety of a municipality uh that change in the law in 2014 also broadened the criteria somewhat the two most common that um have been utilized before 2014 and for that matter are still often used uh relate to the age of leisure the housing stock that it's 50 years old or more so west orange would almost certainly qualify if the majority of the housing stock is at least 50 years old and likewise a majority of the water and sewer infrastructure would be at least 50 years old and in need of repair or substantial maintenance so those are commonly uh utilized criteria there are some others regarding environmental contamination and some other factors but if any one of those criteria are met there's six criteria in all including the two i meant i called out u

m you you can designate an area or an entire municipality as in need of rehabilitation so what's the the basic the benefit for this the designation provides municipalities with essentially all of the powers that you have for redevelopment areas with a couple of exceptions and they're big ones condemnation is not available it's not permitted with an area in need of rehab likewise long-term tax exemptions or what are known as pilots payments in lieu of taxes are also not permitted under an area in need of rehab so i know that's often a concern this designation again would not allow for that so essentially though any other power that you have under the redevelopment law with regard to preparing redevelopment plans entering into redevelopment agreements with developers transferring property without bidding those types of things will be permitted also in a redevelopment plan uh the the planning board and council can prepare plans that ar

e more detailed in terms of design requirements uh with rather regard to green infrastructure or pedestrian improvements uh even building materials things like that benches and streetscape you can get as detail as you want in a redevelopment plan which the town cannot do through zoning which is the typical way of regulating land use and development uh so the process uh allows also for five-year tax exemptions or abatements i know uh mayor parisi mentioned this at the the rock springs presentation roughly a month ago but this is a tool that is i think the reason many municipalities are starting to take advantage of a town-wide designation uh because you can again it's not a it's not the typical 30-year or long long-term tax abatement but for a five-year period if the ordinance is passed to allow for it it can affect commercial properties residential properties industrial properties all of the above uh depending on how it's structured

i'm not an expert on the details of the the financing aspects of it but again it is a tool that would allow the municipality to reward essentially homeowners for improving their properties by giving them a break on you know the taxes on the value of the improvements so this is um that i think that that and the redevelopment plan being able to be adopted are two of the primary benefits uh as was mentioned as well according to my research and you can go to the department of community affairs website from the state it has a listing of the towns that have taken advantage of town-wide designation there are at least uh over a dozen in northern new jersey alone i just focused on the northern new jersey area they include both urban and suburban settings as well as even some small towns and more rural areas so in essex county for example belleville bloomfield south orange as well as newark and irvington all her area in need of rehabilitation across the entire municipality uh you

4,720 also have more suburban places like metuchen and westfield uh places like dover and high bridge which are you know smaller towns or more remote locations throughout the state um so there's again there's a full listing that i believe was provided at the a previous council meeting on the rock spring presentation but again it's something that is not just what you find in you know isolated you know urban settings but it's being applied in municipalities even such as west orange which have a more suburban type environment and are not typically thought of as being in need of rehabilitation um so in closing on my direct presentation i think it's a realistic option certainly for west orange in terms of the housing stock and or the water and sewer infrastructure it's over 50 years old and you just need to report to uh to essentially confirm that and provide the evidence uh to meet those criteria and it would provide another tool for regulating

8:35,279 development as well as promoting investment again there's a lot of the options that would be on the table uh typically for redevelopment other than condemnation and pilots all those other options would theoretically be on the table for the area that's designated uh so i know i've presented a lot of information uh i'd be happy to uh you know go back to anything or to answer any specific questions that the uh the council and others may have thank you thank you mr gregor mr mayor mr president again just to kind of focus i know that was quick and we went through a quick couple weeks ago and obviously there'd be a lot more discussion the council would want to entertain but the the big takeaway from the administration wanting to pursue this is is the advantages to property owners uh in encouraging them to renovate their homes so one of the things we hear most often as i'm sure council members do as well is when people renovate their homes all of a sudden their taxes go up because 310 00:09:32,160

they've increased the value now that's not a west orange problem it's a new jersey problem and what is a very um prohibitive tax system um frustrating tax system based entirely on value and when someone puts a lot of money into their home it unfortunately increases the value and regrettably uh increases the taxes so the area in need of rehabilitation on a town-wide basis provides homeowners a five-year tax break on the added assessment so especially when we're looking at a unprecedented strength in the real estate market all over jersey all over this region our hope would be to encourage residents to invest in their homes and this is another another way that would encourage them because they would not be saddled with a increase in property taxes just based on the improvements that they made so i know there's a lot of information that the council is going to want and pursuing this and discussing it further but we think that's a significant benefit 3

to our tax to our taxpayers for property owners and something that we think is worth considering and we'd hope the council would consider it as well thank you mr mayor i do have a few questions and then i'll ask my council colleagues um to share their questions if they have um as well um i i know during the last presentation of the raw spring i did ask and i don't know mr greigel if um you are aware whether or not this designation for the whole municipality um impacts state aid um to the municipality at all it's my understanding it does not it's a separate process so it doesn't you know put you into a different category okay great um and then you mentioned um the two big exceptions that um no pilots um and no condemnation so that means my understanding when you said no pilots and please clarify uh for me that's no pilots at all at any time as long as the designation is in place or am i misunderstanding it's okay maybe i wasn't quite clear yes

pages.text: this is to inform the general public that this meeting is being held in compliance with section 5 of the open public meetings act chapter 231 public law 1975. the annual notice was emailed to the star ledger and filed in the township clerk's office on november 30th 2020 and published in the west orange chronicle on december 10 20 20. councilwoman casolino present councilwoman mccartney present councilman rutherford president councilwoman williams president council president matthew brown present thank you very much and welcome everyone to our tuesday evening council meeting on our conference agenda we have a proclamation reading and i'm going to ask councilwoman casolino to please introduce the proclamation yes thank you very much this evening we have our library director david qb who is here we wanted to take a moment to honor his staff for the tremendous draw job that they have done this past year

pages.text: this is to inform the general public that this meeting is being held in compliance with section 5 of the open public meetings act chapter 231 public law 1975. the annual notice was emailed to the star ledger and filed in the township clerk's office on november 30th 2020 and published in the west orange chronicle on december 10 2020. councilwoman casolino present councilwoman mccartney president councilman rutherford president councilwoman williams president council president matthew brown president thank you very much madam clerk and thank you everyone for joining us on our council meeting of july 13 2021 we are in our consent agenda and in this portion we do have a congressional update by our congressman donald payne i don't believe he has joined us yet so i am going to open the floor at this time and introduce a young man from our community who is the executive director of the young 34 00:01:16,64

dems in west orange baharam who's been working to and has had meetings with the chief of police to discuss the status and um of the civilian review board as they have advocated for here in west orange so um because they have had discussions and the chief has updated uh the council or the mayor um i i certainly wanted to provide the opportunity for bahrain to come on and share with the council so that when when we are presented with an ordinance that we have a better understanding of the why and the the purpose so welcome bara thank you for joining us please feel free to introduce yourself as well uh thank you uh as a council president to brown said i am bahar muratu i am the executive director of western young dems and i do a lot of stuff within the town and i'm here to talk about a uh an ordinance of the civilian review board do you mind if i share my screen um deb can you can you let him share this uh screen please i'm so sorry thank you are

you able to do that now um uh yes thank you i don't know thank you let me just reload the page real quick uh sorry about that that's okay um all right perfect so a civil review board an integral leads to a better west orange so to first understand the importance of a civilian view board we have to look at the data on the national level so on average one thousand civilians are killed by law enforcement each year this is taken from the washington post and this is taken from an average of each year since 2015 and following that trend since january the start of this year there have been 469 police shootings that means on average there has been 2.5 police shootings each year i mean each day and as you can see uh following the graph we're well on our way to reach 1000 and that is a large issue uh in 2020 alone there were 1021 police shootings now manji this is a time where majority of us work within our homes where we weren't having any

interaction with anybody that we knew much less police officers so the fact that 1021 there were 1021 police shootings in 2020 a year where we were majority of us were in our homes where majority of us had little interaction with the public that is very alarming in 2020 alone there were only 14 days where there wasn't a police shooting now it gets worse when you actually look about how minority populations are affected by this so in a study conducted by the proceedings of the national academy of sciences it was found that black black citizens latinx and american natives slash alaska natives are more likely to die to police compared to their white counterparts on average black citizens are 2.5 times more likely in latin x citizens are 0.5 times more likely to be shot and killed by an officer as you can see there's a huge disproportionate rate of the killings of minorities specifically black minorities compared to the majority which is

the white population minorities are 2.5 times more likely to be killed as you can see there's a huge spike compared to the average rate at which the white population is killed compared to the black population this is a huge issue that's been permeating through this nation since the founding of this nation and it's an issue that we need to address now that leads us to the question how do we address this issue the answer is a ccrb so first off what will a ccrb do it will first investigate the the main point of ccrb a civilian complaint review board is to investigate complaints against officers who violate the rights of citizens or practice any sort of misconduct this can for example be excessive force um harassment uh demeaning the meaning language uh abusive language so say racial slurs uh attack uh attacking somebody because like escalating the situation to somethi

ng that shouldn't have been two it'll build trust through a ccrb community members will feel more at ease around officers through these investigations we'll be able to find officers who are who have inherent biases against certain populations who have a track record of violating their duty to protect and serve who use their uh who use their position to attack instead of protect so this will build trust within the community because we understand that the officers that we have within the department are the best of the best that we don't have any bad officers and uh finally it'll empower the public prior uh many times i've talked to many people about this a lot of citizens citizens didn't feel like they had any power to address if they were violated by an officer if they were harassed by an officer because they had no ability to go up to an officer and say you did wrong to me i want justice sentences will be able to address officers who violate proper conduct and ensure that justice prevails 191 00:07:

now when it comes to a ccrb we have to be very specific about what we need in a ccrb for what for one we need equal representation representation of the whole as a community for so such as um east orange i believe they had a ccrb which had appointees from the naacp um and multiple other organizations aclu pretty much organizations that appoint people who look for an equal unbiased view of an officer's actions and while i say equal representation i also mean officers as well it's customary that a ccrb has someone such as the police chief on a on the board this provides equal defense for the officer who's being accused of misconduct and this also provides a clear a more clear detail and more understanding from both sides of civilians and officers two recommendation of disciplinary actions um so as a cecrb it is an investigative uh and see it is not a

37,760 means to punish officers or direct disciplinary actions uh these will only be recommendations for disciplinary actions the municipal municipality or whatever governmental body does take it upon themselves to carry out these disciplinary actions we'll be in charge of that what the ccrb will do is recommend a specific disciplinary action that fits the actions of the officer uh point number three majority civilian appointees so to ensure that there is not any unfair bias towards an officer the ccrb should be comprised of majority civilians when i say unfair bias i mean in the sense that benefit of the doubt or anything of that sense civilians officers um they'll be able to kind of take their own uh their own experiences and put it into the situation while we take civilian uh civilian experiences and put that into the situation this will put in uh into perspective of both sides so as to understand as to come together to a proper uh into a proper uh action members of the public uh these 254 00:09:

appointees will be members of the public who have little to no interaction with officers in their professional lives and finally point number four subpoena power this is possibly the most important point of the ccrb that we need a civilian complaint review board needs subpoena power in order to function as an entity a civilian complaint review board without the ability to subpoena will only be a symbolic gesture and not a true vehicle for change we do not need a civilian view board that is a civilian review board in name only without subpoena power the civilian review board can't work it's an investigative body and without subpoena power it can't investigate thus that's why we need a supplementary board with investiga with subpoena power so it can investigate now finally to my last slide to emphasize the importance of a ccrb um i bring you to these three men i believe that we've all seen them on the news on in newspapers the times huffington post whatever

00:10:50,880 um in the case of casey gutson jr he was shot and killed by ex-deputy sheriff jason mead casey goodson jr was entering his home his back turned to deputy sheriff jason mead with a subway sandwich in his hand so that he could feed his younger siblings deputy sheriff jason mead took this as a gun a sandwich that we all know of being covered in a white paper with green and yellow font something that i believe many of us would not mistake for a gun ex-deputy sheriff jason mead referred to his job as hunting and was quoted as saying that he loves it and also before referred to the use of force as righteous release that is a huge issue it should not the the position of officers should not be viewed as a hunting a job to hunt people it should be viewed as a job to protect and serve which i believe many officers agree with next we have ex-officer daniel pantalia this officer was the same officer who put eric garner in the chokehold as he

0:11:59,440 pleaded for his life and said i can't breathe prior to the murder of eric garner pantaleo had 12 complaints made against him by citizens complaints that went unheard and again hearing those faithful words of i can't breathe it brings us to george floyd the same words uttered by george floyd as ex-officer derek shelvin put his neck put his knee upon george floyd's neck prior to the murder of george floyd chavin had 18 complaints made against them this is a large issue these were all preventable cases if it was taken upon the municipality of these specific uh towns they could have seen that these officers were a risk to the community and to other officers as well ex-ops ex-deputy sheriff jason mead uh was also to say that if one officer starts hitting if one officer throws a punch he's joining in he considered his time off his temporary suspension as a vacation day he viewed his job first

and foremost as an excuse to fight that should not be that should not be the main thought of an officer his main thought should be to protect and serve these were all officers whose main thought was to use their position to hold it against somebody else and sadly because of that mentality these officers took the lives of innocent bystanders innocent people who didn't do anything wrong to merit death um with that said i do hope that we can have a we can implement a ccrb within west orange so that issues such as derek siobhan daniel pensen leo and ex-deputy sheriff jason mead do not exist again thank you alright thank you so much for the work that you and your colleagues have taken um the time um you've put forth to researching and meeting with our chief of police i see our chief was honest he's still here i'm glad that you're here chief as well so that can you provide an update as to where we are we've had these discussions before um but more recently can you just gi

ve us a more recent update as to where we are with um moving forward so we're going to need a a council ordinance which um i'm going to work with the legal department and and go ahead and run with because i i there's a lot of restrictions um as uh as barack said you know the subpoena power um i i don't know that i disagree with subpoena power is just not uh legally permissive right now so i think you know i should uh work along with the legal department and my staff and i and get what we can do in place because i although you know there can be a better system down the road when the law permits um i still think you know doing something is better than doing nothing so you know if we move in that direction we can always you know consider this a work in progress and get to where we down the road um there's a there's some uh legislation pending um that uh i noticed john mckean was actually uh one of the co-sponsors had signed on to that would allow for subpoena power but

i don't know where that is in that bureaucracy down in trenton right now so you know if that passes that would in able to uh enable ccrb to have subpoena power um you know i've been in many conversations with the command staff at work and you know they understand it this is what the community wants and it's far better to be a part of this process than to uh you know to not be a part of it and have it uh forced on you and um you know it just it just makes us look more out of touch and i don't i don't want to give the impression that command staff feels differently than i do that's not the case but they understand that and i you know i explain to them that's the message that they have to you know filter downward to their people that you know this is important this is what the community wants is what the people in the whole united states for that matter seem to want and you know we we need to be a part of that not apart from it thank you chief for all of your work and

think all of you know that i'm an associate professor of sociology at john jay college of criminal justice but for the past um two decades i've been working on public health and public health related issues and i've had a great opportunity to be a grantee of the robert wood johnson foundation for my own personal research academic research but i've also been supported by the robert wood johnson foundation and rwj barnabas when they combined to be able to lead our young people in public health research and in 2017 rwj barnabas with the help of micheline davis and barry ostrowski allowed our girls to be able to embark on an internship where they had a chance to um to really learn from and shadow leaders all across um the rwjf system so they did an impover policy behavioral health services of all different sorts and at the end of it they had a capstone course where they had to talk to the foundation about what they saw was missing and in that process they discovered that in all the work that they had done over

0:03:58,959 basically um a hundred hours is that people were not talking about prevention enough and they weren't talking about issues of prevention as it relates to youth and those young people from that project came up with green ribbon week it isn't an idea that come from me it really comes directly from young people who are between 10 and 15 years old and many now are between 16 and 19 years old and what they discovered was that with so much that is happening around health and well-being is that we pay a lot of attention to crises is that we are equipped to deal with things once they're broken but they're very little efforts being made to deal with things in terms of sustaining their health and well-being and they wanted to have a week devoted to really helping young people recognize that they need to pay attention to their wellness and they wanted that week to also be a week where adults begin to recognize that they could be more supportive of young people especially in these transitional moments and they chose september because sept

ember is such a big transition for young people it's often when people are changing grades it's often when they're going off to college it's often when these big moments happen so it's really i'm here on behalf of those young people who found gra um green ribbon week four years ago and rwj barnabas adopted this policy because it really struck them that preventative care is something that is not often discussed so the evolution of green ribbon week i wanted you to know that history and what it has really come towards now is a week devoted to listening and breathing so having asking adults to take the time to listen to young people without interruption all of the social science evidence that tells us that when young people share their ideas with a caring adult and it's uninterrupted that opportunity allows young people to feel cared for and many of us feel like we care for our young people all the time but the concept of being cared for is really in the beholder's eye so if young people share something with you and they feel 150 00:06

like you're not listening then really they really cannot benefit from what you have said the other concept is breathing we know that our as young people they are impulsive at times and are apt to take actions without great thought and experience and so we ask him this week for young people to breathe and talk it out and we're hoping on the other side that adults will listen west orange high school has adopted this policy for the past four years and they've also won at the at the state level several awards for adopting green ribbon week so it is a five-day process and we have a day of solidarity where we ask everyone to wear green they chose green and green is also grassroots colors because these girls were from it but green really emphasizes the earth and in that week we also asked young people to spend a lot of time in nature the research our public health research the scientific and psychological research tells us that when people spend at least 10 minutes outside they actually have improved and preventative health and so in the 178 00:07:

we can do to make sure that they are well and so green ribbon week is our opportunity to say look there might be many things that are happening on your mind that we can actually pause take a minute to listen and also that we can teach young people that they need to tune into themselves and not wait until there is a crisis so i am hoping that the town will consider adopting green ribbon week as our philosophy in the sec the third week september 20th to 24th of september where all of us can come together and start to think about mental wellness instead of waiting until there is a suicide there's an attempted suicide or some other action that we can think about our wellness in the same way our mental wellness in the same way that we think about our physical health and well-being so i hope you will consider this adoption um so that we can come together as a town and begin to see that this is this moment where we can prioritize our mental health um instead of waiting until there is a crisis thank you so much fo

pages.text: okay this is to inform the general public that this meeting is being held in compliance with section 5 of the open public meetings act chapter 231 public law in 1975 the annual notice was emailed to the star ledger and filed in the township clerk's office on november 30th 2020 and published in the west orange chronicle on december 10 2020. councilwoman casolino present councilwoman mccartney present councilman rutherford here councilwoman williams president council president matthew brown president thank you so much everyone um and thank you to the public who is here joining us for our tuesday october 12 um 2021 council meeting wanted to start um before we get started with the consent agenda i just wanted to read into the record and i will do this moving forward until the end of my presidency a land acknowledgement as we start this meeting we recognize that we reside on the land of the lenape people and we honor and give thanks to

them for the stewarding of these lands for generations i also wanted to thank the mayor for his proclamation i'm not sure if everyone had the opportunity to um listen to his proclamation in his statement on social media which was shared but i also wanted to share it herein in the spirit of reconciliation i encourage all citizens of the township of west orange today to take time today to celebrate indigenous people of our region including the nanticoke leni lenape nation the pahaotang nation and the raritan nation to name a few since their first contact with europeans people indigenous to new jersey found their lives and lands relegated to the despoiling and dehumanizing forces of oppression violence and discrimination from colonial expansion to systemic modern marginalization we recognize that our indigenous people have too often been denied practical access to the resources and benefits of our society in recognition of that stark history i call upon us all to reassess geographic 63 00:02:31,599

place place names statutes monuments and to fully reflect our state's inclusive values to research and formally recognize ancestral connections to public and private lands throughout our region to embark upon the difficult journey of truth-seeking in hope of finding healing reconciliation together and to squarely face the complete history of our indigenous peoples and recognize the impact that our nation's history has had upon their culture and lives i call upon our educators parents elected officials and community leaders to incorporate knowledge of and respect for our indigenous peoples in our schools hearts and commemorations i call upon the state of new jersey to take action to convey ancestral lands back to tribes for preservation of cultural resources therefore be proclaimed that henceforth the second monday of october shall be forever known as west orange as indigenous people's day in the township of west orange i really wanted to thank the mayor um for his uh recognition

0 um you know we are working toward a more um racially inclusive community here and and everywhere also want to acknowledge his efforts um and participation in supporting um both the hispanic heritage month that occurred uh it's still occurring actually october i'm sorry september 15th through october 15th um and to all of my colleagues for their participation as well and also want to acknowledge um italian heritage month which began october 1st and i was happy to see that the township was able to support the efforts of unico with the italian flag raising um in collaboration with some of my council members that my council colleagues that were able to attend unfortunately i was in trenton still at five o'clock and was not able to make it home quick enough from work but i am happy to see that our township at least is looking at inclusive um community building and acknowledgement because it starts with each one of us i also have on the another uh proclamation from the mayor

for the metastatic breast cancer awareness day and hashtag lightupmbc campaign october 20 20 i'm sorry october is breath cancer awareness month whereas october is breast cancer awareness month let it be known that the township of west orange is pleased to recognize and observe october 13 2021 as metastatic breast cancer awareness day and hereby recognizes the hashtag light up mbc national campaign whereas breast cancer is the most common type of cancer among women in the world and the second leading cause of cancer death among women in the united states more than one in eight women and one in 833 men in the u.s will be diagnosed with breast cancer in their lifetimes in 2021 an estimated 284 200 americans will be diagnosed with invasive breast cancer including over 8 330 women in new jersey whereas metastatic breast cancer occurs when breast cancer spreads to other parts of the body including the bones lungs liver and brain and has an average life expenses expectancy of 26 months regardless of early

detection approximately 30 percent of stage 0 to 3 breast cancer will return as stage 4. an estimated 44 100 americans including 1 250 new jerseyans will die from breast cancer in 2021 equal to 115 women and men per day with 98 due to metastatic breast cancer and whereas the national organization met survivor research and support funds critical stage 4 metastatic breast cancer research educates the public about metastatic breast cancer and lack of funding for stage 4 treatment they aim to dramatically increase the current percentage of u.s breast cancer research dollars from under 5 to 30 for the already metastasized patient the national hashtags for this initiative on social media fall under hashtag don't ignore stage 4 and light up mbc whereas there is a national hashtag light up mbc campaign on october 13th every year to illuminate 115 landmarks in the metastatic colors of teal pink and green throughout the world bringing awareness to the disease and to honor the

daily number of lives lost to nbc in partnership between the meta survivor and more fight more strong organizations the day will culminate in virtual broadcasting hashtag light up nbc live to communicate landmarks lighting around the country share inspiring stories of the metastatic breast cancer community and raise research funds whereas the pink ribbon is well known for representing the fight against early stage breast cancer it is not inclusive of stage four therefore the metastatic breast cancer awareness tri-color ribbon includes teal pink and green the teal color portrays healing and spirituality green represents the triumph of spring over winter life over death renewal hope and immortality and the thin pink overlay signifies the cancer originated in the breast whereas metastatic breast cancer affects all races and socioeconomic classes while caucasian women see slightly higher incidences incidence rates of breast cancer the mortality rate for black women with breast cancer is 41 212 00:08:47

percent higher than that of caucasian women and breast cancer is the leading cause of cancer-related death for hispanic women now therefore be it resolved that the township of west orange hereby encourages encourages citizens to join the national effort towards awareness of metastatic breast cancer during october through metaviber thank you to all for your patience and listening and i will now open the floor to my council colleagues for their liaison announcements um councilwoman casolino thank you council president welcome appreciate all that uh i had to start off by thanking our downtown director megan brill and our west orange public works department they did an outstanding job this past sunday for the mayor's 5k walk unfortunately the weather was not outstanding uh but all the volunteers that came out i really we really appreciate it uh it was pouring at one point in the morning when we were setting up and we want to thank the runners who c

ame out the weather had cleared up for them uh so good time was uh was had by all uh with the elements uh but we appreciate that and we gotta thank the food pantry because for all the service they've done and and the downtown will be presenting them uh with the with the check uh soon uh for them uh council president had mentioned our heritage flag raisings for the italian uh heritage month and for the hispanic foundation and and big thank you to unico and the hispanic foundation uh for uh setting up those events we appreciate it um i'm being all-inclusive with all our residents very important here in the township um senior services has been putting out a lot of information i'm not going to name them all but they've had a lot of great programs and for those who are 55 and older please go to the website or you can call laura van dijk at 973-325-4105 or get a judgement uh noelia and and getting on get on the newsletter there's so many great such

hoping someone in our community would step up and leave dialogue um and i i don't know why we are all so scared of dialogue columbus day may be an unfortunately named holiday but i assure you it was never intended uh to celebrate genocide or white supremacy uh columbus became a mascot for italian americans because our ancestors saw everyone else admiring them or admiring him they came to this country mostly in the 19th century and saw cities and streets named after him our nation's capital the district of columbia is named after him and our ancestors foolishly thought that if they claimed this northern italian men for their own maybe they would finally give us the respect we deserve columbus day is a day to honor ancestors who came here with nothing fought and died in wars and built cities like newark orange and west orange but there is a precedent for erasing some of the contributions of italian americans uh the old first ward in newark uh used to be little italy was bul
